planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Giant Belgium Pink is a large-fruited heirloom beefsteak tomato producing flattened, deeply lobed pink-red fruits frequently exceeding 1–2 lbs, with sweet, rich, low-acid flesh similar in character to Brandywine but from a distinct European heirloom lineage — one of the great large pink beefsteak tomatoes for home garden growing.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after frost danger passes into deeply prepared, fertile, well-drained soil. Giant Belgium Pink (also called Belgian Giant) is an indeterminate heirloom tomato with large, flattened, sometimes deeply lobed, pink to pinkish-red beefsteak fruits that frequently weigh 1–2+ lbs, rivaling Brandywine in size and beauty; the flesh is dense, meaty, sweet, and low-acid with a rich complexity suited for fresh slicing and sandwich use. The fruits share the large-pink-beefsteak class with Brandywine and Mortgage Lifter, providing an additional option with slightly different flavor character for gardeners who prize this class of tomato. Indeterminate; stake or cage as plants reach 5–8 feet; productivity is good for a giant beefsteak but individual fruits require time to develop fully. Outstanding for Gulf Coast spring tomato production planted February through March for May–June harvest.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Pruden's Purple is a large, potato-leaved, purple-pink beefsteak tomato saved by William Pruden of Roanoke Rapids, North Carolina, closely related to Brandywine in flavor and character but often producing more reliably and slightly earlier — considered by many tomato enthusiasts an excellent alternative or companion to Brandywine.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 2 weeks after last frost into fertile, deeply prepared, well-drained soil. Pruden's Purple was saved by William Pruden of Roanoke Rapids, North Carolina and entered the Seed Savers Exchange network in the 1980s; it is a large, indeterminate, potato-leaved (smooth, undivided leaf form, as in Brandywine) beefsteak tomato producing very large (12–24 oz), flattened, lobed, dusky rose to purple-pink fruits with dense, meaty, sweet, complex flesh with the rich, full tomato flavor characteristic of the best pink beefsteak heirlooms. Many tomato growers consider Pruden's Purple slightly more productive and reliably earlier than Brandywine Sudduth while producing nearly identical flavor; the two make excellent companions in the garden. Days to maturity of 65–80 from transplant make it one of the earlier large beefsteak heirlooms. A consistently excellent performer in Southern and Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Speckled Roman is a distinctive paste-type heirloom with dramatically striped red-and-orange fruits and equally striped leaves, producing meaty, flavorful paste tomatoes with more complex flavor and better fresh-eating quality than standard smooth-skinned paste varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; provide substantial staking or caging as plants grow 5–7 feet tall. Speckled Roman tomato was discovered by Doreen Howard in Illinois and is distinguished from virtually all other paste tomatoes by its dramatically striped appearance — the fruits have alternating red and orange stripes (or speckles when young) that persist to full ripeness, making them visually striking among the usually uniformly red paste types. The foliage is similarly striped, with green and yellow banding on the leaves. Beyond the ornamental appeal, Speckled Roman produces exceptionally flavorful paste tomatoes with more complexity and better texture for fresh eating than standard San Marzano or Roma types while retaining the meaty, low-moisture flesh needed for sauce and paste production. Medium-large (4–8 oz), cylindrical to elongated-oval fruits. Outstanding for both fresh use and preserving, and a beautiful conversation piece in the garden.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Bonny Best is a classic 1908 American heirloom slicing tomato — one of the most widely grown varieties in early 20th century American home gardens and commercial fields — valued for its reliable production, attractive round-globe shape, good acid-sweet balance, and relatively early maturity for a full-sized slicer.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Bonny Best performs well with or without staking — it is an indeterminate but moderately compact type compared to most indeterminate heirlooms. Bonny Best was introduced in 1908 (previously distributed as 'John Baer') and rapidly became one of the most widely grown tomatoes in early 20th century America, prized for reliable, heavy production of attractive, round to slightly oblate, medium to large (6–10 oz), uniformly red fruits with a classic balanced sweet-acid flavor that home gardeners and canners found superior to the watery commercial varieties of the era. Bonny Best's particular virtues are its productivity, disease resilience, and flavor — a reliable old-fashioned tomato that consistently produces good crops without fuss. By midcentury it was eclipsed in commerce by hybrid varieties but remained in home garden cultivation; it is now maintained by seed savers and available from heritage seed companies as one of the historically important American tomato varieties. Good canning and fresh-eating tomato.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Dr. Wyche's Yellow is a large golden-yellow beefsteak heirloom named for Dr. John Wyche, a circus owner and celebrated tomato grower from Durant, Oklahoma who maintained an extensive heirloom tomato collection, producing sweet, mild, low-acid fruits of excellent quality for gardeners who prefer yellow tomatoes.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Dr. Wyche's Yellow is an indeterminate variety requiring substantial staking or caging as vines grow 5–8 feet. Dr. Wyche's Yellow was named for Dr. John Wyche of Durant, Oklahoma — a physician, circus owner, and dedicated heirloom tomato collector whose extensive seed collection passed through the Oklahoma State University seed library and into the broader heirloom seed preservation community. The large (10–24 oz), oblate to round, golden-yellow fruits have a beautiful amber-gold coloring that intensifies to deep orange-yellow at peak ripeness; the flesh is sweet, mild, and pleasantly low in acid — characteristic of yellow and golden tomato varieties and particularly appreciated by those who find red tomatoes too acidic. The flavor is rich and complex for a yellow variety, with a honey-melon sweetness that makes it outstanding fresh but equally good roasted or in yellow tomato sauce. One of the finest large yellow beefsteak heirloom varieties available.
